turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion in the Court this morning in case 17-1657, Mission Product Holdings v. Tempnology.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This is a case about the meaning of rejection. It makes it sound a little bit more interesting than it is. Really it is a case about the meaning of rejection under Section 365 of the Bankruptcy Code. That section allows a debtor to reject a particular kind of contract and the question is what consequences flow from that action? The case arises out of a trademark licensing agreement. A company called Tempnology manufactured exercise gear and owned some trademarks basically logos and labels that it used to identify the apparel. It entered into a contract with another company called Mission Products which allowed Mission Products to distribute the apparel using the various trademarks. Everything went along fine for a while but then Tempnology entered bankruptcy and it invoked a part of the Bankruptcy Code Section 365 that allows a debtor to reject a contract like the one here where both parties still have duties to perform, and the question as I said before is what does it mean to reject a contract in those circumstances? What follows from that rejection? There are two possibilities. Tempnology argues that when it rejected the contract it essentially blew the whole deal up. It terminated the contract and put the parties back in the position they were in before it was ever signed. That would mean that Mission would no longer be able to use the trademarks that the contract had conveyed. Tempnology does concede that Mission would have a claim for damages stemming from the rejection, but because Tempnology is bankrupt that claim probably would not amount to much. So it would not surprise you to learn that Mission has a different view. Mission thinks that when a debtor rejects a contract in bankruptcy it is just as if someone outside of bankruptcy had breached a contract and what happens then is that the breach does not terminate the whole agreement, instead the innocent party, the party that has not breached, gets to continue using all the rights that the contract conveyed. So here that would mean that even though Tempnology rejected the contract Mission would still be able to use Tempnology's trademarks. Today we hold that Mission is right. I am not going to go through the analysis jot by jot, it puts a lot of weight on the text of Section 365 and it reinforces that textual analysis by looking to some fundamental principles of bankruptcy law. Most notably it invokes the idea that a debtor in bankruptcy cannot possess anything more, cannot have any greater rights than a debtor had before entering bankruptcy. Outside of bankruptcy as I said, a breach of the contract would not terminate the contract. Even when a breach occurred the innocent party could continue to use the rights the contract had conveyed. So in bankruptcy the same result follows. Because the court below held the opposite we reverse its judgment. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion and Justice Gorsuch as filed a dissenting opinion.
